citations.narrative:
There is one Bactrian camel, one zebra, one zedonk, one llama and one alpaca housed in an outdoor pen without public
barrier fencing or a facility attendant present. The animals walk directly up to the primary enclosure fence, which is made
of widely spaced pipe that can easily allow the passage of human hands or animal heads to facilitate direct contact.
Public barriers or facility attendants are important to protect both the animals and people; people or animals can be
injured by unsupervised, direct contact and animals can become ill if they eat food or objects presented to them by
people. The licensee must ensure that there is sufficient distance and effective barriers of sufficient height and integrity
between the animals and the general viewing public whenever a responsible, knowledgeable, and readily identifiable
employee is not present during periods of public contact.

Non-human primate enclosures are not adequately cleaned:
**The outdoor concrete floor for one adult male bonnet macaque called "Petey" has an accumulation of feces.
**The indoor portion of the shelter for one adult male capuchin named "George" has an accumulation of dried fecal matter
on the bars of the enclosure and smeared on an elevated platform.
Soiled surfaces that are not adequately cleaned can harbor harmful microbes and encourage microbial growth, attract
pests and vermin, and can lead to animal illness. The licensee must ensure that surfaces with which non-human primates
come in contact are spot-cleaned daily and sanitized in accordance with Section 3.84 of the Animal Welfare Act and
Animal Welfare Regulations to prevent accumulation of excreta or disease hazards.

Housing pens are not maintained to ensure they do not cause harm to animals:
**There is a large outdoor pen housing one adult domestic pig that contains a shelter constructed of wood and sheet
metal. There are multiple places where the sheet metal is torn or otherwise has sharp edges that could come into contact
with the animals.
**There is a bent fence panel with grid wire lying on the ground inside one corner of the pen to which the Bactrian camel,
zedonk, zebra, llama and alpaca have access; the spaces in the wire panel are large enough for a foot or hoof to pass
through.
Sharp edges and points can injure animals, and fencing panels on the ground can pose an entrapment hazard. The
licensee must ensure that housing facilities are structurally sound and maintained in good repair to protect the animals
from injury and to contain the animals.

**There is a large pile of rotting vegetables near the pens housing one Bactrian camel, one zedonk, one zebra, one llama,
one alpaca, two raccoons, two foxes, one bobcat and one pig.
**There are several piles of wire, wood, discarded equipment and other rubble in various places, including next to the
rotting vegetable pile, and in front of the pen containing three ducks and the enclosures for three bobcats, one great
horned owl, one red-tailed hawk and two coatimundi.
**The refrigerator and freezer in the kitchen area used to hold perishable food items intended for consumption by animals
is unsanitary and contains food items that are stored inappropriately. The freezer has a dried layer of opaque, pink
material on the floor and contains a turkey that is uncovered, with a desiccated appearance. The bottom drawer of the
refrigerator contains raw beef that is brown in color and leaking tan fluid out of the packaging. There is wrinkled produce
sitting in the leaked fluid and decaying, green vegetable matter.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/24/2026

Although there are a few rodent traps seen in a nearby room, there are accumulations of mouse droppings in numerous
places on the floor throughout the kitchen area. Plastic storage totes for food and enrichment in this area also have
mouse feces on them.
Wild rodents can harbor or spread parasites and disease-causing microbes that can contaminate food and food prep
areas and cause injury or illness to the animals. The licensee must establish and maintain a safe and effective program
for the control of insects, ectoparasites, and avian and mammalian pests so as to promote the health and well-being of
the animals.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 04/17/2026

citations.desc: Environment enhancement to promote psychological well-being.
citations.narrative:
At the time of inspection, the facility could not produce a documented environmental enhancement plan for avian species.
This plan is required to promote psychological well-being of the avian species at the facility. The facility must develop,
document and follow a species appropriate plan for environmental enhancement adequate to promote the psychological
well-being of birds. The plan must be approved by the attending veterinarian and, at a minimum, must address social
grouping, environmental enrichment, special considerations, restraint, and exemptions to the plan.
TO BE CORRECTED BY 03/31/2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n
